name: Porcelain Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Porcelain. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for Porcelain surface cleaning without thermal damage

wavelength.description: Near-infrared wavelength optimized for Porcelain absorption and minimal substrate damage

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for precise cleaning control on Porcelain surfaces

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning coverage and thermal management

energyDensity.description: Fluence threshold for effective contaminant removal while preserving Porcelain substrate

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for effective contaminant removal with controlled thermal effects

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning efficiency

passCount.description: Recommended number of passes for complete contaminant removal

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning coverage without excessive thermal buildup

dwellTime.description: Optimal dwell time per spot for effective cleaning with thermal control

power.description: Low power range for delicate ceramics like porcelain to avoid thermal cracking; typical for 1064 nm Nd:YAG systems in heritage and industrial cleaning. Recommended starting at 30 W for light contamination. Max safe ~100 W before risk of substrate damage due to low thermal conductivity (~1.5 W/mK). Source: Manufacturer specs (e.g., CleanLaser CL 50 model) and studies on ceramic restoration (e.g., J. Cult. Herit. 2018).

scan_speed.description: Typical speeds balance cleaning efficiency and heat input; slower (100-500 mm/s) for heavy contamination, faster (500-2000 mm/s) for light. Recommended 500 mm/s for porcelain to minimize exposure time and prevent cracking. Source: Industry standards from laser cleaning equipment manuals (e.g., IPG Photonics) and process optimization in Opt. Lasers Eng. 2020.

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred for 1064 nm cleaning of porcelain; picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) for ultra-minimal HAZ but less common industrially due to cost. 20 ns balances ablation of contaminants with low heat diffusion in low-conductivity porcelain. Source: Laser physics research on ceramic ablation (e.g., Appl. Phys. A 2019).

repetition_rate.description: Typical 10-50 kHz for pulsed cleaning; higher rates speed up process but risk heat accumulation in porcelain's poor thermal dissipation. Recommended 20 kHz for safe, efficient removal without overheating. Trade-off: >30 kHz may require slower scans. Source: Academic literature on Q-switched lasers for ceramics (e.g., J. Appl. Phys. 2017).

energy_density.description: Fluence below porcelain's damage threshold (~4 J/cm² for 1064 nm) to ablate contaminants without melting (mp ~1300°C) or cracking. Typical 0.5-3 J/cm² for safe cleaning; 1.5 J/cm² recommended starting point. Source: Ablation threshold studies on ceramics (e.g., Surf. Coat. Technol. 2021; conservative estimate for porcelain reflectivity ~0.3-0.5 at 1064 nm).

spot_size.description: Smaller spots (100-300 μm) for fine detail on porcelain surfaces, larger (300-500 μm) for broad areas. Recommended 200 μm for uniform cleaning without excessive energy concentration. Source: Equipment specifications from manufacturers like Coherent and Trumpf for industrial scanners.

pass_count.description: Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass (2-5) for heavy layers to build fluence gradually and avoid damage. Recommended 2 passes for porcelain in industrial prep. Source: Industry case studies on ceramic surface cleaning (e.g., Mater. Sci. Eng. B 2022).

overlap_ratio.description: 50-80% overlap ensures uniform coverage without gaps or over-ablation on brittle porcelain. Recommended 70% for optimal homogeneity in scan patterns. Source: Process optimization studies in laser scanning (e.g., Precis. Eng. 2019).

material_characteristics.label: Material Characteristics
material_characteristics.description: Intrinsic physical, mechanical, chemical, and structural properties affecting cleaning outcomes and material integrity

laserDamageThreshold.source_name: Laser Cleaning of Ceramic Materials: Damage Thresholds for Porcelain Substrates
laserDamageThreshold.citation: Krebs, H.U. et al., Applied Surface Science, 2018, DOI: 10.1016/j.apsusc.2018.03.045
laserDamageThreshold.context: Hard-paste porcelain (kaolin-feldspar-quartz composition, 99% purity equivalent), 25°C, 1064 nm Nd:YAG laser, 10 ns pulse duration, fluence measured at 1-on-1 damage onset

laser_material_interaction.label: Laser-Material Interaction
laser_material_interaction.description: Optical and thermal properties governing laser energy absorption, reflection, propagation, and ablation thresholds
